# Streaming 2025: a deep dive into consumers' changing behaviors

## Tubi's last report shows that 80% of viewers say they’d rather watch a TV show or movie than scroll on social media, and 59% turn to streaming when they need a mental break. On average, Americans now spend $129 per month on a combination of streaming and paid TV services, a 7.5% increase from the previous year.

by [Señal News](https://senalnews.com/en/authors/senal-news)

<news@senalnews.com>

[@senalnews](https://twitter.com/senalnews)

[@senalnews](https://www.facebook.com/senalnews)

[@senalnews](https://www.linkedin.com/company/senalnews/)

19 MAR 2025

Share

[**Tubi**](http://www.tubitv.com)**, Fox Corporation’s ad-supported streaming service, released findings from 'The Stream 2025: Audience Insights Shaping Streaming.' In this year’s report Tubi, who partnered with The Harris Poll to conduct research, provides a deep dive into the changing streaming behaviors of consumers, especially Gen Z, and how marketers can reach these audiences amid the shifting entertainment landscape.**

*“Tubi has built a deeply engaged audience by providing a trusted, premium streaming experience where viewers can freely discover content that speaks to them—without barriers or subscription fees,”* **said Cynthia Clevenger, Senior Vice President of B2B Marketing at Tubi.** *“Today’s viewers are more selective than ever, carefully choosing where to invest their time and attention. As consumer behaviors continue to evolve, these insights offer marketers a valuable opportunity to connect with key audiences—like Gen Z—in an environment where they find real value through diverse content, seamless ad integration, and a viewing experience that mirrors paid services without the cost.”*

**One of the clearest takeaways from the analysis is that viewers are willing to accept ads—but only under the right conditions. While 81% of consumers see ad-supported streaming as a fair trade-off for free content, 79% expect a completely ad-free experience when they’re paying for a service.** This divide highlights the challenge for streaming platforms experimenting with hybrid pricing models, as consumers remain firm in their demand for transparency and value.

Beyond pricing, streaming has become a dominant form of escapism. **A significant 80% of viewers say they’d rather watch a TV show or movie than scroll on social media, and 59% turn to streaming when they need a mental break. Music follows as the second-most popular form of relaxation (50%), while only 38% of respondents rely on social media for downtime.** These trends suggest that streaming services hold a powerful position in shaping modern entertainment habits.

However, the rising cost of streaming is pushing many consumers to reevaluate their subscriptions. **On average, Americans now spend $129 per month on a combination of streaming and paid TV services, a 7.5% increase from the previous year. More than half (56%) carefully monitor their streaming expenses, with Gen Z proving to be the most budget-conscious: 76% say they have canceled or would cancel a subscription due to a price hike.** These findings indicate that as streaming services continue to raise prices, they risk losing cost-sensitive audiences who prioritize affordability over platform loyalty.

When it comes to content preferences, viewers are showing increasing enthusiasm for independent productions over remakes. **The study found that 70% of streaming audiences want more indie films and series, a 4% increase year over year. Gen Z, in particular, is leading this trend—73% prefer watching original content over franchises or remakes**, and 72% wish they had more influence over the types of content being produced for streaming. This shift signals a potential challenge for studios that continue to rely on reboots and sequels to drive engagement.

Interestingly, workplace streaming is on the rise, with younger generations embracing multitasking—at least in theory. **Half of Gen Z admitted to streaming content while working from home,** and nearly 48% acknowledged lying to their bosses about it. As remote work remains a norm for many, it’s clear that entertainment consumption has found its way into work hours, raising questions about productivity and workplace policies in an increasingly digital-first environment.

Nostalgia-driven streaming is another major trend reshaping viewing habits. **Two-thirds (66%) of respondents say they enjoy discovering content that was originally released over a decade ago, and 87% of Gen Z agree that streaming services should surface older titles alongside new releases.** This preference for "new-stalgia" suggests that classic shows and films remain highly valuable assets for platforms looking to retain and engage audiences.

On the advertising front, Gen Z’s engagement with streaming ads depends heavily on relevance. **While 81% of Gen Z viewers say they would consider taking action after seeing an interesting ad, 73% feel that the ads they currently encounter on streaming platforms do not align with their personal interests.** This discrepancy highlights an opportunity for advertisers to refine their targeting strategies to improve ad effectiveness.

**Overall, Americans remain committed to streaming, with 57% watching TV and movies for 1-3 hours per session and 38% streaming for over three hours. On average, viewers juggle nearly seven streaming services, including 3.9 paid platforms and 2.6 free services.** As the competition among streaming providers intensifies, understanding consumer behaviors, pricing sensitivities, and content preferences will be key to securing long-term loyalty.

The findings underscore the need for streaming platforms to balance cost, content, and convenience. As audiences demand more control over their viewing experiences, the industry must adapt—offering a mix of original content, smarter ad strategies, and flexible pricing models that cater to an increasingly discerning audience.

Understanding Viewer Behavior in the Streaming Era: 2024
--------------------------------------------------------

![Image 1](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/7e116e5ac1474791b78387a939b3baf27e3ad93c35b80cd9241bd5d8c195f6e2?s=62&d=mm&r=g)

anantadi
Author

July 30, 2024
Published

Reading

![Image 2: Understanding Viewer Behavior in the Streaming Era 2024 By anantadi](https://i0.wp.com/anantadi.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Understanding-Viewer-Behavior-in-the-Streaming-Era-2024.jpg?fit=1024%2C389&ssl=1)

In 2024, the streaming landscape continues to evolve at a breakneck pace. As content creators, marketers, and platform developers, understanding viewer behavior is crucial for success in this dynamic environment. Let’s dive into the key factors influencing how audiences consume streaming content in 2024.

### 1.The Shifting Sands of Streaming Consumption

#### Multi-Platform Viewing: The New Normal

Today’s viewers aren’t tied to a single device. They seamlessly switch between smartphones, tablets, smart TVs, and laptops, expecting a consistent experience across all platforms. This behavior demands that streaming services optimize for various screen sizes and operating systems.

#### Binge-Watching vs. Weekly Releases: A Delicate Balance

While binge-watching remains popular, some platforms are experimenting with weekly release models to build anticipation and foster community discussions. The success of shows like “Succession” demonstrates that there’s still appetite for the traditional TV release format, even in the streaming era.

#### Niche Content Platforms on the Rise

As the market saturates, we’re seeing a surge in platforms catering to specific interests, from horror movies to documentaries. These niche services are carving out loyal audiences by offering curated content that major players might overlook.

### 2.Demographic Shifts Driving Change

#### Gen Z and Millennials: The Streaming Natives

Younger viewers, having grown up with on-demand content, show distinct preferences. They value personalization, interactive features, and are more likely to engage with short-form content alongside traditional long-form shows.

#### Older Generations Embrace Streaming

Interestingly, 2024 has seen a significant uptick in streaming adoption among older demographics. This shift is driven by user-friendly interfaces, nostalgia-focused content, and the convenience of accessing a vast library of classic shows and movies.

### 3.Content is King: What Viewers Want

#### Original Content Reigns Supreme

Exclusive, high-quality original content continues to be a major draw for subscribers. Streaming giants are investing heavily in producing diverse, innovative shows and movies to stand out in a crowded market.

#### The Nostalgia Factor

Reboots, revivals, and spin-offs of beloved franchises are hitting a sweet spot with viewers. This trend taps into nostalgia while introducing classic properties to new generations.

#### Global Content Goes Mainstream

International content is breaking language barriers. Shows like the Korean drama “Squid Game” have paved the way for a more globalized content landscape, with viewers increasingly open to subtitled or dubbed foreign language productions.

### 4.Technology Shaping Viewer Habits

#### 5G: Boosting Mobile Streaming

The widespread adoption of 5G networks has revolutionized mobile streaming. Higher speeds and lower latency mean viewers can enjoy high-quality video on the go, leading to an increase in mobile viewing time.

#### Smart TVs: The Hub of Home Entertainment

Smart TVs have become central to the home streaming experience. With built-in apps and voice control features, they offer convenience that’s driving cord-cutting trends even further.

#### AI-Driven Personalization

Artificial Intelligence is getting better at predicting viewer preferences. In 2024, we’re seeing hyper-personalized content recommendations that keep viewers engaged and reduce choice paralysis.

### 5.The Social Streaming Experience

#### Second-Screen Engagement Soars

Viewers are increasingly using social media as a “second screen” while streaming, discussing shows in real-time and engaging with bonus content.

#### Virtual Watch Parties

The pandemic-era trend of virtual watch parties has evolved into a mainstream feature. Platforms are integrating social viewing options, allowing friends to watch and interact together, even when physically apart.

#### User-Generated Content Tie-Ins

Streaming services are tapping into the creativity of their audience, encouraging user-generated content related to popular shows. This strategy boosts engagement and creates a sense of community around content.

### 6.Ad Consumption in the Streaming World

#### The Rise of Ad-Supported Tiers

As subscription fatigue sets in, many viewers are opting for cheaper, ad-supported plans. This shift is prompting advertisers to create more engaging, less intrusive ad experiences.

#### Interactive Advertising Takes Center Stage

2024 has seen a boom in interactive ads that allow viewers to engage directly, from exploring product details to making purchases without leaving the streaming platform.

### 7.Subscription Strategies and Viewer Loyalty

#### Subscription Stacking Reaches New Heights

Viewers are subscribing to multiple services to access their favorite content. This behavior is driving competition and innovation among platforms to become “must-have” subscriptions.

#### Battling Churn with Personalization

Reducing subscriber churn is a top priority. Streaming services are using data analytics to predict and prevent cancellations, offering personalized content and incentives to keep viewers hooked.

### 8.Subscription Strategies and Viewer Loyalty

#### Balancing Act: Personalization vs. Privacy

Viewers are becoming more aware of data collection practices. Successful streaming platforms in 2024 are those that can offer personalized experiences while respecting user privacy concerns.

#### Regulatory Impact on Data Usage

New privacy regulations are shaping how platforms collect and use viewer data. This is leading to more transparent practices and giving users greater control over their information.

### 9.Looking Ahead: The Future of Streaming

#### Industry Consolidation on the Horizon

As competition intensifies, we may see more mergers and acquisitions in the streaming space. This could lead to fewer, but more comprehensive, streaming options for consumers.

#### Creator-Driven Content Models

The line between content creators and platforms is blurring. We’re seeing new models emerge where popular creators have more control over production and distribution, potentially disrupting traditional studio systems.

##### Conclusion: Adapting to the Ever-Changing Streaming Landscape

Understanding viewer behavior in 2024 is more crucial than ever for success in the streaming industry. From technological advancements to shifting demographic preferences, the key to thriving in this dynamic environment is adaptability.

For content creators, this means staying attuned to audience preferences and leveraging data to inform production decisions. For streaming platforms, the focus should be on providing seamless, personalized experiences across devices while respecting user privacy.

As we look to the future, one thing is clear: the streaming era is far from reaching its peak. By keeping a finger on the pulse of viewer behavior, industry players can position themselves to ride the next wave of innovation in digital entertainment.

## Research / Insights on current and emerging industry topics

# Streaming consumers like episodic content on big screens

![](https://digitalcontentnext.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Freevee.png)

The streaming industry is experiencing rapid, continuous evolution including consumer behavior and preferences. NPAW’s, a global video analytics company, new report [Video Streaming Industry Report H1 2023](https://npaw.com/download/video-streaming-industry-report-h1-2023/), tracks and analyzes video consumption, consumer behavior, and quality of experience. The research looks at streaming behavior in Asia, Europe, Latin American, North America, and the Pacific. The report shows that overall playtime for the streaming industry is growing steadily, with a 4% increase compared to the previous year. This offers a positive indicator of user engagement with platforms and their content.

## Streaming playtime grows

Further, all regions except Asia report higher playtime per user and service. North America leads the way with a remarkable 41% year-over-year increase, followed by Latin America at 21%. These differences highlight the diverse streaming preferences that exist globally.

![](https://digitalcontentnext.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/global-viewing-chart-1024x711.jpg)

Examining the raw figures reveals the emergence of two distinct categories. Europe, North America, and the Pacific region constitute high-consumption zones, each averaging over 79 minutes of daily service usage per individual. In contrast, the second group, comprising Asia, Latin America, and the Middle East, experiences less usage, with a daily average of under 53 minutes. Notably, Asia records a mere 30 minutes.

## Episodic content takes center stage

![](https://digitalcontentnext.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/share-of-total-playtime-by-type-300x209.jpg)

NPAW’s streaming data shows a clear preference for episodic content among users. Globally, episodes account for 60% of the total playtime, whereas movies capture 25%. This preference for episodic content remains consistent across regions, except in North America, where movies share a comparable share of playtime. Notably, the Middle East stands out as a region favoring episodic content, with movies and short content comprising less than 10% of the total playtime.

## Smart TVs dominate the viewing landscape

The dominance of large screens, especially smart TVs, in VOD consumption, is expanding. In the first half of 2023, smart TVs contribute 51% of the total VOD playtime, highlighting viewers’ inclination towards the immersive experience provided by these bigger devices. This trend also plays a role in consolidating smaller-screen devices like smartphones, which are currently witnessing a decline in their share of playtime.

Parallel to VOD, larger screens continue to dominate linear TV consumption. TVs and set-top boxes collectively account for approximately 80% of the linear playtime, highlighting viewers’ preference for the traditional, in-home viewing experience.

## Sports streaming takes off

One of the most remarkable trends in streaming is the rapid growth of sports streaming. Major sporting events in the first half of the year increased substantially in streaming adoption and engagement compared to the previous year. This surge in sports streaming highlights the changing nature of entertainment consumption as viewers increasingly turn to streaming platforms to catch their favorite sports action.

## Streaming evolution

The streaming landscape continues to evolve in the first half of 2023. VOD consumption continues to grow, driven by user preference for episodic content and larger screens, particularly smart TVs. Improvements in visual and audio quality contribute to a more gratifying streaming journey across most geographical areas. Understanding user preferences and expectations remains pivotal in shaping the future of entertainment consumption. Be it indulging in episodic narratives via smart TVs or immersing in live sporting spectacles on expansive screens, streaming is a universal practice.

DESCRIPTION: The chart displays household engagement levels across various media channels, comparing solar data with Experian Marketing Data, showing a shift towards streaming TV and digital media.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Audiences increasingly favor home viewing, driven by on-demand access and binge-watching options, reflecting a shift towards personalized and convenient entertainment experiences.

enriched_points.point_title: Impact of Social Media on Engagement
enriched_points.expanded_content: Social media acts as a 'second screen,' enhancing viewer engagement through real-time discussions and interactive content, creating a dynamic community around streaming shows.
As streaming platforms proliferate, content saturation poses challenges, leading viewers to become selective, prioritizing quality and relevance over quantity in their viewing choices.
